narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.40 and an LSAT score of 38, you've clearly put in strong effort to build a competitive profile for law school, including planning to attend full-time and coming from a middle-income family background. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your probability of failure at 65%, tipping the scales toward rejection of your application. This prediction was driven primarily by your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, which falls well below the typical student's average of around 5.5 and had the strongest pull toward forecasting a bar failure. Your Year 1 decile of 5, also slightly under the average of 5.7, added some additional concern in the same direction, while your middle family income bracket contributed modestly to the risk assessment compared to higher-income peers. On a brighter note, your LSAT score, above the dataset average of 36.7, pushed meaningfully against failure, as did your undergraduate GPA exceeding the typical 3.21—both standing out as strengths relative to most applicants. Your full-time enrollment plan offered a small positive nudge too, aligning with the vast majority of students who pursue that path. Overall, your profile compares favorably to averages in test scores and GPA, but the lower Year 3 performance stood out as the biggest outlier dragging the prediction down when weighed against typical successful bar takers. We understand this is disappointing, especially given your above-average metrics in key areas that many applicants struggle with. To strengthen future applications, consider ways to boost your later academic deciles, perhaps through targeted study habits or additional coursework that showcases improvement. You might also explore programs offering bar prep support or connect with mentors who can highlight your resilience. Keep pushing forward—your LSAT and GPA are real assets that position you well with some refinement. We're rooting for your success.
